question.label: Self-read opener
question.name: Before I share my view, how do you think the last stretch has gone?
question.description: Anchors the conversation in their self-assessment first; gap vs manager's view becomes a signal.

answer: I can name wins, but I am not always sure they match the team bar.
note: He can identify wins but explicitly says he is unsure whether they meet the team bar, which is a strong clarity gap with mild engagement signal.

question.label: Team bar mismatch
question.name: When your wins miss the team bar, where does the mismatch usually show up?
question.description: Drills into his stated uncertainty about the team bar by locating the recurring mismatch in concrete work.

answer: Feedback has helped, though I still miss consistency under time pressure.
note: He names a recurring quality gap — inconsistency under time pressure — which confirms a strong clarity shortfall around the team bar in practice.

question.label: Time pressure consistency
question.name: Under time pressure, what usually slips first in your work?
question.description: Drills into the specific failure mode behind his inconsistency so feedback can anchor on observable evidence.

answer: I sometimes focus on polish and lose sight of the core message.
note: He names a specific clarity failure mode: under pressure he optimizes polish over the core message.

question.label: Core message miss
question.name: When the core message gets lost, what are you not clear on in that moment?
question.description: Drills into the specific clarity breakdown he just named so the evidence is concrete.

answer: I want clearer examples of what excellent junior-level work looks like here.
note: He names a specific absence — concrete examples of excellent junior-level work — as the clarity gap behind the miss; [THREAD-DEFERRED] Wants clearer examples of excellent junior-level work.

question.label: Feedback changed what
question.name: What changed in your work after the last round of feedback?
question.description: Checks whether prior feedback translated into behavior, not just agreement.

answer: When reviews are broad, I am not sure what to change first.
note: He says broad feedback leaves him unsure what to change first — strong growth blockage with a clear clarity problem.

question.label: Broad reviews gap
question.name: When reviews are broad, what guidance is missing most for you?
question.description: Pins down the specific gap in feedback so the performance issue is named concretely.

answer: I would like to grow faster in concept rationale, not just execution speed.
note: Names a specific missing development area — concept rationale over execution speed — which is a strong growth-gap signal and mild clarity signal.

question.label: Concept rationale block
question.name: Concept rationale — what makes that harder for you right now?
question.description: Gets at the cause behind the stated growth gap in concept rationale.

answer: A tighter loop on one skill at a time would help me improve.
note: Names a missing improvement structure — a tighter one-skill feedback loop — which is a strong growth deficit and mild clarity gap in how to improve.

question.label: Next step agreement
question.name: Given what we've covered, where do you want to focus first?
question.description: Closes on a concrete commitment without reopening new topics.

answer: If we align on concrete criteria, I can work against them confidently.
note: He names concrete criteria as the lever for moving forward, signalling improved clarity and a constructive, confident next-step orientation.

question.label: Next step agreement
question.name: What should we agree you will do before we next meet?
question.description: Closes on a concrete commitment without reopening new topics.

answer: I will start by writing one explicit priority rule I will use when time pressure hits this month.
note: He named a specific behavioural commitment under time pressure, indicating clearer focus and forward movement.
